evidence_text: The study qualitatively analyzed 401 open-source repositories containing cursor rules.
source_excerpt: “This paper presents a large-scale empirical study to characterize this emerging form of developer-provided context. Through a qualitative analysis of 401 open-source repositories containing cursor rules...”
note: The source frames the study as large-scale and empirical, but this evidence unit only captures the stated study scope.
caveat: The source frames the study as large-scale and empirical, but this evidence unit only captures the stated study scope.

evidence_text: The authors organized developer-provided context into five high-level themes: Conventions, Guidelines, Project Information, LLM Directives, and Examples.
source_excerpt: “...we developed a comprehensive taxonomy of project context that developers consider essential, organized into five high-level themes: Conventions, Guidelines, Project Information, LLM Directives, and Examples.”
note: This unit captures the reported taxonomy outcome, not whether the taxonomy is exhaustive beyond this dataset.
caveat: This unit captures the reported taxonomy outcome, not whether the taxonomy is exhaustive beyond this dataset.

evidence_text: Initial repository fetching found 487 repositories with at least one .mdc file before cleaning.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.1 Data Collection and Preprocessing
source_excerpt: “We fetched repositories containing at least one cursor rule file (i.e., with the .mdc extension), excluding any forked or archived repositories, which resulted in 487 repositories.”
note: This is the pre-cleaning repository count.
caveat: This is the pre-cleaning repository count.

evidence_text: The final analysis set was reduced to 401 repositories after multiple manual cleaning steps.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.1 Data Cleaning
source_excerpt: “This left us with 418 repositories for further analysis... we removed 12 such repositories after manual review. Additionally... we removed five such repositories after manual review, leaving 401 repositories for further analysis.”
note: The final dataset depends on manual exclusions for deleted files, non-rule .mdc uses, non-English files, duplicates, and template/example repositories.
caveat: The final dataset depends on manual exclusions for deleted files, non-rule .mdc uses, non-English files, duplicates, and template/example repositories.

evidence_text: The 401 repositories contained 1,876 .mdc files, averaging 4.68 .mdc files per repository.
source_excerpt: “Among all repositories, we identified a total of 1,876 .mdc files. On average, each repository contained 4.68 .mdc files (SD = 6.89), with the maximum number of .mdc files in a single repository being 56.”
note: This is a dataset composition metric for collected rule files.
caveat: This is a dataset composition metric for collected rule files.

evidence_text: 93.27% of repositories placed cursor rule files in the recommended root .cursor directory.
source_excerpt: “We found that 374 repositories (93.27%) followed this recommendation.”
note: The recommendation referenced is placing cursor rule files in a dedicated .cursor directory at the repository root.
caveat: The recommendation referenced is placing cursor rule files in a dedicated .cursor directory at the repository root.

evidence_text: The average cursor rule file length was 462.67 lines, with the longest file at 11,076 lines.
source_excerpt: “The average length of all cursor rule files was 462.67 lines (SD = 1,197.00), with the longest file containing 11,076 lines.”
note: This evidence describes observed file length, not effective context size after exclusions in coding.
caveat: This evidence describes observed file length, not effective context size after exclusions in coding.

evidence_text: The initial open coding sample used 30 repositories, selected as 3 repositories from each of 10 popular topics.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 Qualitative Analysis
source_excerpt: “We then selected the top 10 most popular topics... To prevent class imbalance, we randomly selected 3 repositories from each topic, resulting in a total of 30 repositories.”
note: This unit captures the sampling design for initial open coding.
caveat: This unit captures the sampling design for initial open coding.

evidence_text: Independent coding of the initial 30 repositories by two researchers reached Cohen’s Kappa of 0.71.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 Qualitative Analysis
source_excerpt: “Finally, independent coding of the initial 30 repositories by two researchers yielded substantial agreement... (Cohen’s Kappa = 0.71).”
note: This metric reports inter-rater agreement for the human coding stage.
caveat: This metric reports inter-rater agreement for the human coding stage.

evidence_text: The full-dataset coding used gemini-2.5-flash-preview-09-2025 with temperature 0.0, repeated labeling three times, and majority vote per line.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.3 Qualitative Analysis
source_excerpt: “We used gemini-2.5-flash-preview-09-2025 with temperature 0.0 and dynamic thinking enabled. To enhance reliability, we repeated the labeling process three times and selected the majority vote as the final label for each line. If all three labels were different, we marked it as ‘No Code’ to indicate uncertainty.”
note: This evidence matters to interpretation because part of the coding pipeline depended on LLM-assisted labeling and an uncertainty fallback rule.
caveat: This evidence matters to interpretation because part of the coding pipeline depended on LLM-assisted labeling and an uncertainty fallback rule.

evidence_text: On the initial 30 repositories, LLM-assisted coding reached Cohen’s Kappa values of 0.64 and 0.65 against the two human raters.
source_excerpt: “To validate the LLM-assisted coding, we ran this process on the initial 30 repositories and found substantial agreement between the LLM and both human raters (Cohen’s Kappa = 0.64 and 0.65)...”
note: These agreement values show partial alignment with human coding, not equivalence; the source later notes possible LLM bias or misinterpretation.
caveat: These agreement values show partial alignment with human coding, not equivalence; the source later notes possible LLM bias or misinterpretation.

evidence_text: 2.1% of all lines had three different LLM labels and were marked “No Code,” and the authors say this should be treated as a lower bound.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 3.4 Threats to Validity
source_excerpt: “If all three labels were different, we marked it as ‘No Code’ to indicate uncertainty. There are 2.1% of all lines fall into this disagreement. However, considering the size of our dataset, this number should be treated as a lower bound.”
note: This is an uncertainty indicator in the coding process and may understate total disagreement.
caveat: This is an uncertainty indicator in the coding process and may understate total disagreement.

evidence_text: Project context appeared in 85% of repositories, Guideline in 89%, Convention in 84%, and LLM Directives in 50%.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Table 1 and Sec. 4.4
source_excerpt: From Table 1: “Convention... 0.84”; “Guideline... 0.89”; “Project... 0.85”; “LLM Directives... 0.50.” Section 4.4 also states: “Only about 50% of repositories (compared to 85% for Project, 84% for Convention, and 89% for Guideline) contain LLM-specific instructions...”
note: These are repository-level prevalence rates for top-level categories.
caveat: These are repository-level prevalence rates for top-level categories.

evidence_text: 37.16% of repositories provided four content categories, and 24.69% provided all five categories including examples.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 5.3 Context Co-occurrence
source_excerpt: “In general, developers were motivated to provide comprehensive context for LLMs, with 149 repositories (37.16%) providing four actual content context categories in their cursor rules and 99 repositories (24.69%) providing all five context categories (including examples).”
note: This unit reports category co-occurrence at repository level.
caveat: This unit reports category co-occurrence at repository level.

evidence_text: The dataset contained 19,917 duplicated lines, equal to 28.70% of all lines across repositories.
citation: Jiang and Nam 2025, Sec. 5.4 Proportion of Duplicated Lines
source_excerpt: “This paper presents a large-scale empirical study to characterize this emerging form of developer-provided context. Through a qualitative analysis of 401 open-source repositories containing cursor rules...”
note: The source frames the study as large-scale and empirical, but this evidence unit only captures the stated study scope.
caveat: The source frames the study as large-scale and empirical, but this evidence unit only captures the stated study scope.
